comments:
Summary
This paper describes BADREX, a practical system implemented as a GATE plugin that identifies long-form/short-form (LF/SF) abbreviation pairs in biomedical text using dynamically-generated regular expressions, annotates them in situ, and can coreference subsequent unpaired short forms to their most recent definition. The implementation is accompanied by corrected versions of two commonly used evaluation corpora (BioText/Medstract) and an evaluation comparing BADREX to several prior systems (Schwartz & Hearst, ALICE, MBA), with competitive or improved precision/recall reported.

Strengths
- Practical, well-engineered tool with immediate applicability; availability as a GATE plugin and GPLv3 code release is valuable to the community.
- The approach is simple, fast (single-pass over sentences), parameterisable, and requires no training data, which is attractive for many pipelines.
- The use of dynamically-generated regexes to find the shortest matching LF substring and configurable discard rules is sensible and explains some gains versus prior heuristics.
- The author corrected and released gold-standard corpora errors, which is a useful contribution that benefits reproducibility and benchmarking.
- The evaluation reports high precision/recall on the corrected corpora and compares to established baselines.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Incremental novelty: BADREX builds on well-known heuristics (Schwartz & Hearst; Ao & Takagi). The main novelty is engineering (dynamic regex use, in-place annotation, and coreference). This is useful but conceptually incremental.
- Coreference feature is described but not evaluated. The claimed benefit of in situ expansion for downstream semantic typing and coreference is not substantiated experimentally here.
- Evaluation details are thin in places: it is not always clear whether parameters were tuned on the test corpora, how thresholds were chosen, or whether results are averaged across multiple runs or statistically significant. The MBA system was not re-run; reported numbers are taken from the literature, which complicates direct comparison.
- Potential bias from manual corrections: while fixing corpus annotation errors is valuable, manual additions/amendments of markables (e.g. adding 43 markables) could introduce subjectivity; more detail on the correction process and inter-annotator agreement would strengthen trust in the evaluation.
- Error analysis is limited. Examples of typical false positives/negatives, failure modes (ambiguous abbreviations, nested parentheses, punctuation-heavy SFs), and impact of parameter changes (maxOuterWords, threshold) are not given.
- Paper quality/formatting: the provided text contains duplicated paragraphs, garbled table excerpts, stray characters and editorial artifacts that impede readability. These should be cleaned in a final submission.

Suggestions for improvement
- Provide an empirical evaluation of the coreference/expansion feature: measure how often unpaired SFs are correctly linked, and quantify the downstream benefit (e.g. improvement in semantic typing or co-reference resolution accuracy).
- Include a clear description of parameter selection (defaults vs tuned values), and an ablation or sensitivity study showing how maxOuterWords, maxInnerChars, and matching threshold affect precision/recall.
- Add detailed error analysis with representative examples of false positives and false negatives, and discuss strategies to mitigate them (e.g. string-distance metrics as foreshadowed in the paper).
- If possible, re-run all baselines on the same corrected corpora, or at least clarify differences in evaluation methodologies and explain any discrepancies caused by corpus corrections.
- Fix manuscript formatting and eliminate duplicated/gibberish content; present complete evaluation tables and figures with clear captions.
- Consider additional comparisons with more recent or machine-learning based abbreviation detection/disambiguation approaches, or hybrid methods combining regex heuristics with statistical models.

Overall assessment
BADREX is a useful, well-engineered contribution that will be of practical value to researchers and engineers working on biomedical text processing. However, the intellectual contribution is largely incremental, and the paper would be strengthened by evaluation of the coreference feature, more detailed error analysis, clarity on parameter tuning, and cleaner presentation. Given the practical utility and code release, I recommend acceptance for presentation as a poster (or similar), with revisions addressing the clarity and evaluation concerns.
